one_line_takeaway: Super-DeepG reformulates DeepG's LP- and iterative-optimization-based geometric-robustness certification into closed-form, GPU-tensorizable steps, achieving equivalent or better certified accuracy at up to 526x lower runtime, and (on non-geometrically-trained networks) higher certified accuracy than the faster-but-looser FGV baseline.
key_finding: On PGD-trained networks with matched DeepG parameters (Table 2), Super-DeepG matches or exceeds DeepG's certified accuracy while being dramatically faster: on MNIST rotation [-30,30] deg at 1 deg interval, DeepG certifies 98.0% in 210.72 s/image vs Super-DeepG 98.0% in 0.40 s/image (up to 526x faster, and 0.13 s/image with P=10 samples); on CIFAR10 rotation [-10,10] deg, DeepG certifies 65.0% in 2389.70 s/image vs Super-DeepG 65.0% in 7.58 s/image (~315x faster). Against FGV on CGT-trained networks (Table 3), Super-DeepG matches certified accuracy closely (e.g. MNIST 94.2% FGV vs 94.3% ours; CIFAR10 63.2% vs 63.1%) though FGV is faster on these FGV-tailored networks. On networks NOT trained for geometric robustness (Table 4/Observation 3), Super-DeepG substantially outperforms FGV: for CIFAR10 rotation [-10,10] deg, FGV plateaus at 23.0% certified accuracy even as interval size shrinks to 2e-6 deg (443.00 s/image), while Super-DeepG reaches 65.0% certified accuracy in 1.90 s/image. On TinyImageNet (Observation 5/Table 6), for rotation [-5,5] deg Super-DeepG certifies 18.0% of images in 3.73 s/image while FGV runs out of memory (OoM) at small interval sizes. Against PWL (Table 5, MNIST rotation [-30,30] deg), under PWL's own configuration (interval size 6, P=1000, N=14500) PWL is more precise (91.0% vs Super-DeepG's 86.0%, consistent with PWL's reported 92.9% vs 87.8% on their 98-image subset) but Super-DeepG is already faster (1.14 s/image vs 28.3 s/image); with a finer interval size of 1 deg, Super-DeepG reaches 98.0% certified accuracy in 0.40 s/image (config P=10, N=250), outperforming PWL's reported configuration on both accuracy and runtime. An ablation (Figure 4) shows certified accuracy plateaus beyond P=10 sample points for large intervals (6 deg) and is constant from the start for small intervals (0.5 deg).
core_contribution: Super-DeepG is a reformulation of the DeepG algorithm (Balunovic et al. 2019) for computing certified linear bounds on geometrically-transformed images (rotation, scaling, shearing, translation), used as the input abstract domain for formal robustness verification of neural network classifiers. The paper's own contribution has three parts: (1) an analytical, closed-form reformulation of DeepG's Step 1 sample-fitting optimization (originally solved via an LP/Gurobi solver) that is proven equivalent to the original LP solution (proof by contradiction in Appendix B.1, plus enumeration argument in Appendix B.3/Proposition 1 for the multi-dimensional case); (2) replacement of DeepG's iterative DOO (deterministic optimistic optimization) correction procedure in Step 3 with a static, uniformly-meshed, tensorized partitioning scheme (Step 3-bis) that can be computed in one GPU pass instead of an adaptive loop; (3) an open-source implementation (github.com/NoCohen66/superDeepG) integrated as a CustomOp layer inside the auto_LiRPA verification framework, so certification proceeds via standard CROWN/CROWN-IBP once the geometric bound layer is prepended to the network.
threat_model: Not applicable. There is no adversary, attacker capability, or attack surface in this paper. It addresses formal verification of robustness to naturally-occurring geometric perturbations (rotation/scaling/shearing/translation) of camera images, motivated by a safety-critical use case (aircraft runway detection during landing, using the LARD dataset) rather than an adversarial-security use case. The paper explicitly frames the goal as ensuring a safety property (Eq. 1/5), not defending against a malicious input-crafting adversary.
limitations: Authors state DeepG could not be run on the CGT network due to portability issues, so that specific comparison is absent. PWL's own code could not be executed (MILP-solver dependency), so the PWL comparison relies on PWL's published numbers rather than a from-scratch rerun. Semantify-NN was excluded from comparison entirely, citing prior literature (FGV's own paper) showing it is both less precise than DeepG and slower than FGV, rather than the authors' own re-verification. The work currently addresses only image classification, not object detection, though the authors' own motivating application (runway detection) is stated to require the latter as future work. The authors note their own DeepG CPU timings are slower than DeepG's originally reported numbers because of differences in hardware/docker environment, complicating exact speedup comparisons. The method still relies on a Lipschitz-constant estimate that is only an upper bound, not the true supremum, so bounds remain sound but not maximally tight; the paper focuses on scalar (1-D) transformation parameters for the bulk of the method with the multidimensional (translation) case requiring combinatorial enumeration of point subsets, which the paper claims is not a bottleneck for small P and low d but does not evaluate at higher d.

datasets_used: TinyImageNet (VNN-COMP'24 pretrained model, not geometrically trained)
LARD 2.0 (motivating application dataset for aircraft runway detection, referenced but not used in the reported experiments)
quantitative_results: MNIST rotation [-30,30] deg, 1 deg interval: DeepG 98.0% certified in 210.72 s/image vs Super-DeepG 98.0% in 0.40 s/image (526x speedup)
MNIST rotation [-30,30] deg, 6 deg interval: DeepG 86.0% in 48.38 s/image vs Super-DeepG 86.0% in 1.14 s/image (P=1000) and 0.13 s/image (P=10, marked *)
CIFAR10 rotation [-10,10] deg, 1 deg interval: DeepG 65.0% in 2389.70 s/image vs Super-DeepG 65.0% in 7.58 s/image (~315x) / 1.90 s/image (*)
MNIST scaling [-2,2], interval 4: DeepG 98.0% in 4.25 s/image vs Super-DeepG 99.0% in 0.16 s/image / 0.063 s/image (*)
CIFAR10 scaling [-1,1], interval 1: DeepG 69.0% in 248.13 s/image vs Super-DeepG 75.0% in 0.83 s/image / 0.20 s/image (*)
MNIST shearing [-2,2], interval 4: DeepG 98.0% in 4.30 s/image vs Super-DeepG 100.0% in 0.16 s/image / 0.065 s/image (*)
CIFAR10 shearing [-2,2], interval 2: DeepG 69.0% in 278.29 s/image vs Super-DeepG 69.0% in 0.71 s/image / 0.18 s/image (*)
MNIST translation [-1,1]x[-1,1], interval 0.2: DeepG 98.0% in 460.93 s/image vs Super-DeepG 98.0% in 1.34 s/image

CGT-networks vs FGV: MNIST R(30 deg) FGV 94.2% in 0.0020 s/image vs ours 94.3% in 0.17 s/image; CIFAR10 R(10 deg) FGV 63.2% in 1.04 s/image vs ours 63.1% in 0.97 s/image; TinyImageNet Sh(2) FGV 18.7% in 0.17 s/image vs ours 19.4% in 0.54 s/image; TinyImageNet Sc(2) FGV 15.2% in 0.16 s/image vs ours 15.9% in 0.48 s/image; TinyImageNet R(5 deg) FGV 13.1% in 0.79 s/image vs ours 13.0% in 1.28 s/image
Non-CGT-trained networks vs FGV (CIFAR10 R(10 deg)): FGV plateaus at 23.0% certified accuracy (interval sizes down to 2e-6 deg, up to 443.00 s/image) while Super-DeepG achieves 65.0% in 1.90 s/image
TinyImageNet R(5 deg), non-geometrically-trained: Super-DeepG 18.0% certified in 3.73 s/image vs FGV OoM at interval size 1e-4 deg

PWL comparison (MNIST rotation [-30,30] deg): PWL config IS=6/P=1000/N=14500 gives 91.0% certified (92.9% on 98 clean images) in 28.30 s/image vs Super-DeepG same config 86.0% (87.8%) in 1.14 s/image; Super-DeepG config IS=1/P=10/N=250 gives 98.0% (100.0%) in 0.40 s/image
baselines_compared: FGV (Yang et al. 2022 / Provable defense against geometric transformations)
PWL (Batten et al. 2024, piecewise-linear + Lipschitz optimisation)
Semantify-NN (Mohapatra et al. 2020) - excluded from direct experiments, cited via prior literature as dominated by DeepG/FGV
